claims.text: The GAA c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) variant is described as the most frequent pathogenic mutation in people of Southern Han Chinese ancestry, causing infantile-onset Pompe disease (IOPD).

citations.quote: The c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) variant, the most frequent GAA pathogenic mutation in people of Southern Han Chinese ancestry, causes infantile-onset Pompe disease (IOPD), presenting neonatally with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality.

claims.text: The GAA c.1935C>A transversion is located in exon 14 of the GAA gene and results in the p.Asp645Glu (p.D645E) missense mutation; it is the most frequent pathogenic variant associated with IOPD in Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations of Han ancestry.

citations.quote: Among these variants, the GAA c.1935C>A transversion in exon 14, which results in the p.Asp645Glu (p.D645E) missense mutation, is the most frequent pathogenic variant associated with IOPD in the Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations of Han ancestry, but is not frequently reported in any other region

claims.text: In Taiwanese populations, the c.1935C>A variant represents 36–80% of mutations in IOPD patients, possibly reflecting a founder effect from Southern Han Chinese diaspora.

citations.quote: In Taiwanese populations, this c.1935C>A variant represents 36–80% of mutations11·12, and occur in context of a specific haplotype with conserved polymorphic markers linked to Taiwanese Pompe IOPD patients comparing to normal individuals. This may suggest the existence of a founder effect stemming from a diaspora of Southern Han Chinese to Taiwan and other locations

claims.text: The clinical phenotype caused by GAA c.1935C>A in humans is neonatal-onset IOPD with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality.

citations.quote: presenting neonatally with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality

claims.text: In homozygous GAA c.1935C>A patient fibroblasts, GAA enzyme activity was previously measured at 0.08–0.82% of normal control range.

citations.quote: This aligns with observed levels of low GAA enzyme activity (0.08–0.82% of normal range for control) previously measured in homozygous GAA c.1935C>A patient fibroblasts

claims.text: A Gaa c.1935C>A knock-in C2C12 myoblast cell line showed near-abolished GAA enzymatic activity (~2.3% of wild-type) and increased PAS staining indicating glycogen accumulation.

citations.quote: GAA enzymatic activity was almost abolished in Gaa c.1935C>A KI cells relative to Gaa wt cells; less than 2.3% of WT GAA activity was detected in the KI cell line

claims.text: Homozygous Gaa em1935C>A knock-in mice had approximately 1% of wild-type GAA activity in heart, skeletal muscle, and brain tissue, consistent with near-complete loss of enzyme function from this allele.

citations.quote: KI mice had normal Gaa mRNA levels with significantly reduced level of GAA hydrolysis activity (about 1% of WT) in heart and skeletal muscle, as well as brain tissue

claims.text: Homozygous Gaa em1935C>A mice showed normal Gaa mRNA expression, indicating the missense variant does not cause nonsense-mediated decay.

citations.quote: The comparative ΔCt between mouse Gaa and housekeeping gene Gapdh acquired by RT-PCR among WT, HET, and KI groups are almost identical, indicating the Gaa c.1935C>A mutation does not affect Gaa mRNA levels

claims.text: Homozygous Gaa em1935C>A knock-in mice at 3 months of age exhibited hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (increased IVSd, LVPWd, and LVMI) and reduced forelimb grip strength (~19% reduction in male KI mice) but no premature mortality.

citations.quote: Homozygous Gaa em1935C>A knock-in mice exhibited normal Gaa mRNA expression levels relative to wild-type mice, had near-abolished GAA enzymatic activity, markedly increased tissue glycogen storage, and concomitantly impaired autophagy. Three-month-old mice demonstrated skeletal muscle weakness and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy but no premature mortality.

claims.text: Heterozygous Gaa em1935C>A mice showed approximately 50% of wild-type GAA enzymatic activity and no phenotypic differences from wild-type mice, consistent with autosomal recessive inheritance.

citations.quote: While no phenotypic differences were noted between GAA c.1935C>A HET and WT mice aside from the expected 50% reduction in HET GAA enzymatic activity, the homozygous KI mice demonstrated a significant, PD-like phenotype.

claims.text: The paper classifies the GAA c.1935C>A variant as a pathogenic variant causing IOPD, referring to it explicitly as 'the GAA c.1935C>A pathogenic variant.'

citations.quote: The Gaa em1935C>A knock-in mouse model recapitulates multiple salient aspects of human IOPD caused by the GAA c.1935C>A pathogenic variant.

claims.text: In Southern Han Chinese populations with IOPD, the GAA c.1935C>A mutation represents 36%–80% of mutations, based on multiple references cited in the paper.

citations.quote: In populations of Southern Han ancestry, the GAA c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) mutation represents 36%-80% of mutations in IOPD patients.

claims.text: Homozygous Gaa em1935C>A mice developed hypertrophic cardiomyopathy at approximately two months of age, which became marked by three months of age, with impaired autophagy in skeletal muscle and elevated lysosomal burden in the brain.

citations.quote: Gaa em1935C>A mice developed hypertrophic cardiomyopathy at approximately two months of age, which becomes quite marked at three months of age. This muscle weakness phenotype may be due to a combination of sequelae from cardiomyopathy, impairment of lysosomal-autophagosomal fusion into autolysosomes, and catabolism of myofibril contractile proteins
